coredata.dc:title: “On-Off-On” fluorescence sensor based on g-C3N4 nanosheets for selective and sequential detection of Ag+ and S2-
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Shan
Lu, Qian
Yan, Xu
Yang, Mingming
Ye, Ranfeng
Du, Dan
Lin, Yuehe
coredata.dc:description: Detection of silver (Ag+) and sulfide (S2−) ions is important because their presence in large amounts can cause many diseases. In this study, a novel, simple, “on-off-on” fluorescence sensor based on g-C3N4 nanosheets for sequential detection of Ag+ and S2- was designed. The fluorescence signal of the g-C3N4 nanosheets is quenched because Ag+ chelates with the N of the g-C3N4 nanosheets, leading to photoinduced electron transfer from the sheets to Ag+. After adding S2−, the fluorescence of the g-C3N4 nanosheets is recovered due to formation of Ag2S, which activates the fluorescence of the g-C3N4 nanosheets. The recovery efficiency was found to increase with increasing concentrations of S2-, with linear calibration ranging from 0nmol/L to 30nmol/L. Other potentially interfering species, such as SO4 2−, PO4 3−, HPO4 2−, H2PO4 −, CO3 2−, NO3 −, Ac−, and HCO3 −, presented negligible effects on S2− detection. Moreover, the proposed sensor exhibited several advantages, including low cost, easy preparation, rapid detection, excellent biocompatibility, and a switchable fluorescence response. These attributes make this fluorescent sensor a promising tool for environmental applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: G-C3N4 nanosheets
Fluorescence
Sensor
Ag+
S2−

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: UV–vis and fluorescence properties of g-C3N4 nanosheets

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of g-C3N4 nanosheets sensing to Ag+ and S2-.
XPS survey spectrum of g-C3N4 nanosheet. (a), Full spectrum. (b), C1s spectrum. (c), N1s spectrum.

The UV–vis and fluorescence property of g-C3N4 nanosheets (a), The UV–vis absorption of g-C3N4 nanosheets. (b) The fluorescence emission at the different excitation wavelength. (c), The fluorescence emission and excitation.

Emission spectra of Ag+ (0, 3.3., 6.7, 10, 13.3, 16.7, 20, 23.3, 26.7, 30, 33.3, 36.7, 40, 43.3, 46.7nM) with excitation at 310nm (left). The linearity of peak intensity with respect to Ag+ concentrations (right).

Emission spectra in the presence of S2- (0, 3.3, 6.7, 10, 13.3, 16.7, 20, 23.3, 26.7, 30nM) water solution (CAg +=3.3nM, left). Emission spectra response at 443nm as a linear dependence with the concentration of S2- (right).

Fluorescence emission spectra response of 443nm in the presence of different anions (Canions=3.3nM, CS 2-=23.3nM). The anions from 1 to 8 are SO4 2−, CO3 2−, Ac−, HCO3 -, HPO4 2−, H2PO4 −, PO4 3−, NO3 −.
The analytical performance of various Ag+ sensors.
Comparison of sensing performance of different methods for S2- detection.

head.ce:title: “On-Off-On” fluorescence sensor based on g-C3N4 nanosheets for selective and sequential detection of Ag+ and S2-

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering of Xianyang Normal University, Xianyang 712000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering of Xianyang Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanical and Materials Engineering, Washington State University, Pullman, Washington 99164, United States

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Mechanical and Material Engineering, Washington State University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Mechanical and Materials Engineering, Washington State University, Pullman, Washington 99164, United States.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Detection of silver (Ag+) and sulfide (S2−) ions is important because their presence in large amounts can cause many diseases. In this study, a novel, simple, “on-off-on” fluorescence sensor based on g-C3N4 nanosheets for sequential detection of Ag+ and S2- was designed. The fluorescence signal of the g-C3N4 nanosheets is quenched because Ag+ chelates with the N of the g-C3N4 nanosheets, leading to photoinduced electron transfer from the sheets to Ag+. After adding S2−, the fluorescence of the g-C3N4 nanosheets is recovered due to formation of Ag2S, which activates the fluorescence of the g-C3N4 nanosheets. The recovery efficiency was found to increase with increasing concentrations of S2-, with linear calibration ranging from 0nmol/L to 30nmol/L. Other potentially interfering species, such as SO4 2−, PO4 3−, HPO4 2−, H2PO4 −, CO3 2−, NO3 −, Ac−, and HCO3 −, presented negligible effects on S2− detection. Moreover, the proposed sensor exhibited several advantages, including low cost, easy preparation, rapid detection, excellent biocompatibility, and a switchable fluorescence response. These attributes make this fluorescent sensor a promising tool for environmental applications.

In this study, a novel, simple, and rapid sensor based on graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) nanosheets was designed. the proposed sensor showed several advantages, including low cost, easy preparation, rapid detection, good biocompatibility, and a switchable fluorescence response.
fx1

ce:section.ce:para: The development of sensitive chemosensors, which selectively recognize cations, specifically metal ions, has received significant consideration in the chemistry, biomedicine, and environmental fields [1–7]. Many methods employ techniques related to metal ion sensing. The quenching photoluminescence of carbon-based nanodots has been focused on monitoring metal ions, such as Hg2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, and Cr3+ [8–12].

Silver has attracted considerable interest for its high electrical and thermal conductivities and antibacterial properties. This element has been widely used in industry, agriculture, pharmaceuticals, and food science. Ag+ should be detected for the benefit of environment and public health [13], however, the traditional methods of detection are laborious, costly, and time consuming [14–17].

Furthermore, Ag+ assays can detect other materials, such as sulfide ions. Sulfide ions exist widely in the environment, such as in automobile exhaust, pesticides, industrial waste gases, bleach, and corruption of organic matter [18]. Sulfide also produces numerous diseases, including liver damage and Alzheimer's disease [19]. Therefore, the development of economic, non-invasive, highly sensitive, and selective methods for sulfide ion detection is considered important. Nevertheless, the detection of sulfide ions in aqueous solution is rather difficult. Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) has attracted significant interest in optics because of its high fluorescence, excellent biocompatibility, and nontoxicity [20–22]. Researchers have presented many methods to explore this family of compounds and have obtained promising results.

In this study, g-C3N4 nanosheets served as an effective fluorescent probe. As shown in Fig. 1, g-C3N4 combined with Ag+ via a photoinduced electron transfer, thereby resulting in fluorescence quenching. Its fluorescence intensity was recovered with the addition of S2− to form Ag2S.

Ag(NO3)2, Na(SO4)2, NaHCO3, Na2HPO4, NaH2PO4, NaCO3, NaAc, NaNO3, and Na3PO4 were purchased from Sigma. All chemicals were used without any further purification. Ultrapure water was obtained through a Millipore system.

Ultraviolet-visible light (UV–vis) absorption spectra were obtained using a Thermo Scientific Evolution 300 UV–vis spectrophotometer with 1cm quartz cells. Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra were measured on a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed using a Philips CM200 UT instrument (Field Emission Instruments, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were collected using a PHI Quantum 2000 XPS system (Physical Electronics, USA) with Al Kα radiation (ℏυ=1486.60eV).

g-C3N4 nanosheets were prepared using a chemical cleavage method as follows [22]: 5mL of 0.1M sodium hydroxide, 5.0g of dicyandiamide, and 0.5g of ammonium chloride were mixed and ground for 1h in an agate mortar. Subsequently, the mixture was thermally reacted in a tube furnace at 550°C for 4h, and a yellow-white solid powder was obtained. The powder (20mg) was dispersed in 10mL of water. The dispersion was conducted using an ultrasonic disrupter for 1h. After this process, the mixture was centrifuged at 3000rpm for 10min, and the supernatant liquid was collected to obtain g-C3N4 nanosheets. The as-obtained ultrathin g-C3N4 nanosheet solution is very stable in both the acidic and alkaline environment and shows pH-dependent photoluminescence [23].

The fluorescence detection of Ag+ was conducted at room temperature in water. During atypical experiment, 10μL of the g-C3N4 nanosheet solution was added to 3mL of ultrapure water. Next, a specific amount of Ag+ was added. The fluorescence emission spectra of the g-C3N4 nanosheets were recorded approximately 2min later for a uniform dispersion.

For the determination of S2−, g-C3N4 nanosheets were mixed with water containing 3.3nM Ag+ and different S2− concentrations, to a final volume of 3.2mL. After reacting for approximately 5min at room temperature, the fluorescence spectra were recorded.

The selectivity of the fluorescence of the “on-off-on” system for detection of S2− was assessed by investigating the fluorescence response of other anions. The experiment was performed using a concentration of 3.3nM under the optimum experimental conditions. The concentrations of all substances were the same in the solution containing 3.3nmol/L Ag(I). The detection conditions used were the same as in previous experiments, as well as the ratios (I/I0) for S2− with other interfering species.

Fig. 2 shows the results of XPS analysis. The C–C bond from sp2 C atoms bonded to N in the aromatic ring attributed to defect-containing sp2 hybridized carbon atoms was located at 284.8 and 288.0eV. The N1s XPS spectrum can be deconvoluted into three peaks centered at 397.04 and (Fig. 2c) attributed to the sp2 aromatic nitrogen atoms bonded to tertiary nitrogen N–(Carbon)3 groups. The prepared g-C3N4 nanosheet suspension showed good stability for more than half a year.

The TEM morphology of g-C3N4 nanosheets is presented in Fig. 3. g-C3N4 nanosheets showed low thickness and could be dispersed in water. The g-C3N4 nanosheets, as observed from the TEM (Fig. 3), were thin and small; their diameter was in the range of 50–200nm. The color of the g-C3N4 nanosheet solution was milky white, it and remained stable in storage at 4°C for six months without any aggregation [21]. The inset in Fig. 3 shows the photographs of the g-C3N4 nanosheets under daylight and 365nm UV irradiation. The results clearly indicated good dispersibility and stability of g-C3N4 nanosheets [22].

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–vis and fluorescence properties of g-C3N4 nanosheets
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 shows the UV–vis and fluorescence properties of g-C3N4 nanosheets. A strong peak at 310nm in the UV–vis spectrum can be seen in Fig. 4a. In the fluorescence spectrum (Fig. 4b), only one emission peak exists at 443nm when the excitation wavelength was changed from 280nm to 330nm. When the excitation wavelength was 310nm, the maximum emission fluorescence intensity was obtained. The constant emission wavelength was attributed to the g-C3N4 nanosheet solution containing a single emitter [24]. The strong fluorescence emission at 443nm in Fig. 4c shows that the nanosheets were fluorescent. Notably, the g-C3N4 nanosheets were significantly stable as no floating or precipitated nanoparticles were observed for several months.

Ag+ can chelate with N of the g-C3N4 nanosheets and draw closer together; thus, Ag+effectively quenched the fluorescence of g-C3N4 nanosheets when Ag+ ions were introduced to the g-C3N4 nanosheets(other competitive metal ions for instance Na+, K+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Al3+, Cr3+, Pb2+,Co2+, Mg2+,Hg2+, Zn2+ and Ni2+ (10nM) have hardly responded to the emission of the probe except Cu2+,Hg2+. Only Ag+ resulted in obvious decrease of the fluorescence intensity. This result showed that the g-C3N4 sensor can be used for sensitive detection of Ag+. The conduction band of g-C3N4 nanosheets to Ag+ led to fluorescence quenching because of the photoinduced electron transfer (PET). During the quenching process, Ag+ concentrations in the range of 0–46.7nM were studied. Fig. 5 (left) shows a decrease in fluorescence intensity at 443nm with an increase in Ag+ concentration. The experiment illustrated that the fluorescence intensity of g-C3N4 nanosheets was sensitive to Ag+. Furthermore, the changes in fluorescence intensity at 443nm displayed a linear relationship with the Ag+ concentration ranging from 0nM to 46.7nM. The fluorescence spectra, as presented in Fig. 5 (right), showed good linearity existed between the florescence intensity and Ag+ concentration. The following linear equation described the relationship between fluorescence and Ag+ concentration: Y=−6.7112X+602.50, with a correlation coefficient R2 of 0.9997. The quenched photoluminescence can be described by the Stern-Volmer equation,
(1)
F
0
/ F = 1 +
K
sv
[
Ag
+
]
where [Ag+] is the concentration of the quencher (Ag+) and Ksv is the Stern-Volmer constant. F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of g-C3N4 in the absence and presence of Ag+. The Ksv is calculated to be 3.16×108 M−1. A comparison between the present sensor and other reported sensors for Ag+ determination is summed up in Table 1.

The presence of Ag+ is known to cause fluorescence quenching because S2− effectively combines with many heavy and transition-metal ions to produce a low-solubility M2S or MS species. The reaction between the two ions with strong binding ability forms a stable product, Ag2S, and causes fluorescence enhancement [30].

To demonstrate the“on-off-on” sensing platform characteristics, the fluorescence response in the presence of S2− is presented in Fig. 6 (left). The fluorescence intensity was gradually restored with S2− addition; the S2− concentration ranged from 0nM to 30nM, and the Ag+ concentration was 3.3nM. A good linear relationship over the range of 0–30nM, with a correlation coefficient of 0.9939, was also obtained (Fig. 6, right). Table 2 showed the different methods for S2− detection.

The effect of other potentially interfering species, such as SO4 2−, CO3 2−, Ac−, HCO3 −, HPO4 2−, H2PO4 −, PO4 3−, and NO3 −, was studied to evaluate the specificity of the sensor and is shown in Fig. 7. The results indicated that the sensing system performed with exceptional selectivity for S2−.

The developed optical sensor based on g-C3N4 nanosheets was employed for detection of industrial waste water samples. the RSD was lower than 4.1% and the average recoveries in the real samples were in the range of 97–101%.

A highly selective and sensitive probe was developed by using g-C3N4 nanosheets to detect Ag+ and S2− ions. The PET process caused the quenching of fluorescence intensity of the g-C3N4 nanosheets in the presence of Ag+. In addition, the fluorescence intensity was gradually restored with the subsequent addition of S2−. The reaction between Ag+ and S2− ions with strong binding ability formed a stable Ag2S product and resulted in fluorescence enhancement. Our present study provides an economic, stable, and simple optical method for the simultaneous detection of Ag+ and S2−.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by China Scholarship Council. We also thank Prof. Ming Xian and Dr. Wei Chen at Washington State University for helpful discussion and experiment.
